era_context: Early Bronze Age III. Old Kingdom Egypt (Dynasty IV) under Khafre. Sumerian Early Dynastic Period III in Mesopotamia. Indus Valley settlements expanding. Minoan civilization emerging in Crete. Bronze-working technology spreading across the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East.

events.title: Khafre's reign continues — Old Kingdom Egypt at peak power
events.description: Khafre's reign represents the continuation of Old Kingdom centralized authority and monumental construction. The pharaonic state maintains tight control over the Nile Valley, extracting surplus through taxation and corvée labor to fund pyramid construction and temple building. The administrative apparatus of the Old Kingdom—including the vizier, provincial governors, and scribal bureaucracy—functions at high efficiency. This period marks the height of Old Kingdom artistic and architectural achievement.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Giza and other Old Kingdom sites
Inscriptions from Old Kingdom tombs and temples

events.certainty_note: Khafre's reign is well-attested archaeologically, but precise year-by-year chronology is impossible. Conventional dating places this within his reign (circa 2558–2532 BCE), but absolute chronology is debated.

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the assumption that Khafre's reign can be assigned to specific years in the 3060s BCE is itself uncertain; Egyptian chronology for this period may be off by 50–150 years depending on which chronological system is adopted (high, middle, or low chronology).
historiographic_note: The historical record for 3066 BCE is almost entirely dependent on Egyptian monumental and epigraphic evidence, which is biased toward royal and elite narratives. No contemporary written records from Mesopotamia, the Indus Valley, or elsewhere can be assigned to this specific year. The absence of records from most of the world reflects both the limited spread of writing and the loss of perishable materials over millennia.
